etymology.description: 邻(鄰) (lín, "neighbor") originally depicted ears of grain stacked close together, illustrating proximity. 居(居) (jū, "live") shows a person under a roof, representing habitation.
etymology.interpretation: 鄰(鄰) (lín, "neighbor") reflects people or homes situated close to one another, similar to how stacked grain ears are adjacent. 居(居) (jū, "live") indicates a place of residence. Together, 鄰居 (línjū, "neighbor") describes those who live nearby in relation to one's home.

examples.chinese: 我的邻居(我的鄰居)每天早上都遛狗(每天早上都遛狗)。
examples.pinyin: Wǒ de línjū měitiān zǎoshang dōu liù gǒu.
examples.english: My neighbor walks the dog every morning.

examples.chinese: 新来的邻居(新來的鄰居)很友好(很友好)，我们常常一起聊天(我們常常一起聊天)。
examples.pinyin: Xīn lái de línjū hěn yǒuhǎo, wǒmen chángcháng yìqǐ liáotiān.
examples.english: The new neighbor is very friendly, and we often chat together.

examples.chinese: 邻居家的孩子(鄰居家的孩子)每天都在院子里玩耍(每天都在院子裡玩耍)。
examples.pinyin: Línjū jiā de háizi měitiān dōu zài yuànzi lǐ wánshuǎ.
examples.english: The neighbor's children play in the yard every day.
